I usually check my emails at nine.
Right now, I am checking a new report.
I deal with reports every week.
This week, I am preparing for a big meeting.
Vanessa, what does a typical workday look like for you?
I usually deal with emails first, and by noon I tend to tackle the hard tasks.
And the afternoon? Do you take long breaks?
Hardly ever. I run errands and then I prepare for the next day.
That sounds steady. Is this week the same?
Not really. This week I am working on a special project, so I am staying later than usual.
So your routine is normal, but right now something extra is happening.
Exactly. I constantly remind myself it is only temporary.
I usually start my day by checking my calendar.
During the week I tend to deal with hard tasks early.
I hardly ever run errands on a weekday.
Right now, I am working on a special project.
My routine is steady, but this week is a little different.

Let me walk you through a typical day for me. I usually start my day by checking my calendar and answering a few messages. By noon, I tend to deal with the most demanding tasks, because that is when I have the most energy. During the week I rarely take long breaks, although I occasionally step outside for some fresh air. In the afternoon I run errands or prepare for the next day. Late at night, I finally slow down and read for a while. This is my normal week. Right now, however, things are a little different. I am working on a special project, so these days I am staying later and learning a new tool. It is temporary, but it is exciting. That is the difference: my routine is what I always do, and my project is what I am doing at the moment.

I want to share something about habits and change. Most of us live two lives at the same time. There is the life we repeat every day, and there is the life we are building right now. Your routine is the foundation. You wake up, you work, you rest, and you do it again. It is steady and it is reliable. On top of that, there is something temporary happening: maybe you are learning a language, maybe you are training for something, maybe you are preparing for a big move. In English, we keep these two ideas apart with two tenses. The present simple describes what is generally true. The present continuous describes what is happening around now, for a limited time. When you can hear the difference, you can describe your whole life clearly: the steady part and the changing part, side by side.

First, I wake up early. Then, I usually check my calendar. After that, I deal with my main tasks before noon. During the week I rarely stop for long. Right now, however, I am working on a special project, so I am staying later than usual.
